political_offices.title: Delegate
political_offices.notes: Connecticut had never had a formal constitution — the old charter of King Charles II had served as substitute. At this Convention, Samuel Hart was present along with three of his brothers-in-law: William Cook of Danbury (m. Rebecca Hart), Orin Lee of Granby (m. Charlotte Hart), and Elisha Treat of Middletown (m. Lydia Hart). Hart and two of the three sons-in-law were tolerationists; Orin Lee was on the other side.

character.description: A man of great independence of character, proud spirited, and an earnest and effective speaker.
character.religion: In religion he was a very prominent Universalist.

cross_connections.display_label: Fellow Hooker descendant in same 1757 Fort William Henry alarm.

bio.bio_blurb: Revolutionary fifer, state senator, and Universalist

military_service.notes: Served as a fifer; DAR Ancestor #A051699.

narrative_blocks.body: A man of stern independence and ardent temperament, Samuel Hart was an earnest and effective public speaker, sat in both houses of the legislature, and helped frame the state constitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He fifed through the Revolution as a youth
narrative_blocks.body: Born in 1761, he served as a fifer in the Revolution before becoming a prominent Universalist and state senator from the First District.

career.role: State Senator (First District); member of the legislature, both branches
career.notes: Delegate from Berlin to frame the state constitution; prominent Democrat.

narrative_blocks.header: Overwork unhinged him; he recovered to write
narrative_blocks.body: Cyrus Wadsworth Hart studied law so closely that he became for a time partially insane, then recovered and made his living writing and selling books in Ohio.

bio.bio_blurb: Wealthy Kensington farmer and state legislator

narrative_blocks.header: He farmed two hundred acres and made law
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Hart inherited his father's Kensington homestead, worked two hundred acres as a wealthy farmer, served as justice of the peace, and won a legislative seat in 1837.

bio.bio_blurb: Iowa soldier who died in the war

military_service.notes: Died in service in 1863 at the Island No. 10 post on the Mississippi.

narrative_blocks.header: He died a soldier far from home
narrative_blocks.body: Sherman Hart enlisted in an Iowa regiment and died in 1863 in the war's western theater, at the Island No. 10 post on the Mississippi.

narrative_blocks.header: She carried the family south to Alabama
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy Hart married Henry Russell Lyman and settled at Montevallo, Alabama, where she raised her family and died in 1914.

narrative_blocks.body: Serving as a lieutenant in General Wolcott's detachment, Samuel Hart was wounded at Saratoga on September 19, 1777, and two years later led the Berlin company that turned out at the New Haven alarm.

narrative_blocks.header: A thinker who could not speak unwritten
narrative_blocks.body: A great reader, he could not address an audience without first writing out his ideas; in religion he turned Universalist after a church dispute.

notable.primary_url_label: Berlin in the French and Indian War (p. 13)
notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary captain, father of Emma Hart Willard

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both commanded Connecticut militia in the Revolution.

documents.document_blurb: Emma Willard's 1855 poem names both parents — the most direct portrait of Samuel Hart's character.
Almira Hart Phelps article on the Hart family, NYGB Record.

military_service.unit: Col. Cook's Regiment; Berlin militia company
military_service.notes: Lieutenant under Gen. Wolcott's detachment at Saratoga, wounded 19 Sep 1777; captain of the Berlin company at the New Haven alarm, 1779, repelling Tryon's invasion.
Fifteen days, 8-22 Aug 1757, in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry.

career.role: First Clerk and Treasurer, Berlin Congregational Church
career.notes: Withdrew from the Second Congregational Church in 1807 after a religious dispute.

sources.full: Mrs. Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps, article on Hart family, New York Genealogical and Biographical Record
sources.author: Mrs. Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps (1793-1884), youngest daughter of Capt. Samuel Hart of Kensington, granddaughter of Lieut. Samuel Hart and Mary (Hooker) Hart

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Lieut. Samuel Hart married I00029's wife's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099, b.~1697); she had married Nathaniel Hart in 1719.

One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Lieut. Samuel Hart married I00029's wife's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101, b.~1701); she had married Matthew Hart in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: Died when his only son was fourteen
narrative_blocks.body: Lieut. Samuel Hart had planned a collegiate education for his only son Samuel, but died when the boy was about fourteen. His widow, unwilling to part with him, kept him home and provided the best education locally available.

open_questions.question: Elihu Burritt descent through this line
open_questions.notes: CLOSED: Elihu Burritt's Hooker descent through this line is NOT CONFIRMED. His connection to the tree is through KINSHIP, not descent: his grandfather Elijah Hinsdale (X01931) and Lydia Hinsdale Hart (I00034) were siblings — both children of Capt. John Hinsdale (X00039). This makes Burritt the first cousin once removed of Emma Hart Willard (H00597) and Almira Hart Phelps (H00598). The claim that Burritt descended from Thomas Hooker through Huldah Hart (H00280) could not be traced through available sources. He is documented in the tree as an orbit_notable (X01932) with the Hinsdale kinship connection noted.

sources.author: Mrs. Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps (1793-1884), youngest daughter of Capt. Samuel Hart of Kensington, granddaughter of Lieut. Samuel Hart and Mary (Hooker) Hart

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Nathaniel Hart married Mary Hooker's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099) in 1719; Mary Hooker married I00029 in 1723.

One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Matthew Hart married Mary Hooker's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101) in 1726; Mary Hooker married I00029 in 1723.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.
